scenario: A 35-year-old male presents with muscle weakness 2 hours after lunch. He is a construction worker. PE: hoarseness, muscle weakness grade I/V all extremities, normoreflexia, mild paresthesia in both hands and feet. What is the most likely diagnosis?

choices.label: E
choices.text: Hypokalemic periodic paralysis with thyroid disease

ai_notes: Key clinical features: acute onset paralysis after food intake in young male, normoreflexia (excludes myasthenia which has weakness without reflex loss initially), hoarseness suggests bulbar involvement. Periodic paralysis fits best with acute episodic weakness and association with meals. Hypokalemic periodic paralysis most common type. Added option E as plausible distractor given presentation could include thyroid association.
subject: neuro_med

scenario: A 62-year-old man has proximal muscle weakness that improves with repetitive movement. Chest x-ray is shown. What is the diagnosis?
ai_notes: Proximal muscle weakness that improves with repetitive movement is classic for myasthenia gravis. Thymoma is found in 10-15% of MG patients and is the most common anterior mediastinal mass associated with MG in this age group. Chest X-ray would show anterior mediastinal mass. Note: Original image/X-ray findings not provided; diagnosis inferred from clinical presentation. Added option E as plausible mediastinal mass distractor. Status marked for review due to missing imaging details.
